title: The Ghurid Aftermath
subtitle: a manumitted slave-soldier proclaims himself sultan and begins the Qutb Minar
bullets: The Delhi Sultanate began with a horse accident: Qutb-ud-din Aibak, a Turkic slave-soldier of Muhammad of Ghor, proclaimed himself Sultan at Lahore in 1206 and ruled four years before being killed in a polo accident.

The mamluk slave-soldier system, inherited from the Abbasids, produced soldiers with no kin ties to anyone except the sultan and regularly produced dynasties — Aibak's Mamluk dynasty in Delhi was directly contemporary with the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt founded in 1250.

Aibak built the Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque on the site of twenty-seven demolished Hindu and Jain temples, deliberately reusing their carved columns in the prayer hall as a visible statement of religious supersession.
The Qutb Minar, begun by Aibak in 1199 and completed under his successors over the next century, stands 240 feet tall and remains the tallest brick minaret in the world.

The fourth-century Gupta-era Iron Pillar of Delhi — six tons of nearly pure wrought iron that has resisted rust for sixteen centuries — was preserved intact in the Quwwat-ul-Islam courtyard by every Sultanate dynasty that passed through.

1206 was also the year Genghis Khan was acclaimed Great Khan of the Mongols at the kurultai on the Onon river, a coincidence that would matter when the Mongols started raiding the Sultanate's western frontier within fifteen years.

Aibak's son-in-law Iltutmish was the real institutional builder of the Sultanate, securing investiture from the Abbasid Caliph in Baghdad in 1229, building up the Chihalgani corps of forty senior slave-officers, and introducing the silver tanka and copper jital coinage.

title: Razia and the Slave Dynasty
subtitle: the only female sultan, the iron rule of Balban, and the Mongol threat at the frontier
bullets: Iltutmish nominated his daughter Razia as his successor over his sons, explaining publicly that he had observed her ruling Delhi as his regent and she had managed it better than her brothers ever had.
Razia Sultana ruled the Delhi Sultanate from 1236 to 1240 — the only woman to hold sovereign rule of any major medieval Indian empire and one of the very few in the medieval Islamic world.

She abandoned purdah seclusion, wore men's clothing at official functions, rode horseback in public, and insisted on being called Sultan rather than Sultana — choices that scandalized the Turkic nobility.

Razia was killed in October 1240 outside Delhi after the Forty deposed her in favor of her brother; she is buried in a small forgotten tomb in old Delhi while her father's Qutb Minar draws three million tourists a year ten miles away.

Genghis Khan personally pursued the Khwarazmian prince Jalaluddin Mingburnu to the Indus river in 1221, briefly bringing the Mongol army within striking distance of Delhi before turning back to Mongolia.

Ghiyas-ud-din Balban, originally one of the Forty, took the throne in 1266 and broke the Forty's institutional power through systematic demotion, exile, and selective execution — including the poisoning of his own cousin Sher Khan.

Balban introduced the Sasanian-style Persian court ceremonial of sijdah and paibos prostration, presented himself as the Zilullah or 'shadow of God on earth,' and created a professional intelligence service called the barid reporting directly to the throne.

By the 1280s, Balban's standing army on the western frontier under his cousin Sher Khan kept the Chagatai Mongol raids from reaching Delhi — the institutional foundation for the Khalji defeats of the great Mongol invasions still to come.

title: The Khalji Imperial Moment
subtitle: Alauddin Khalji defeats six Mongol invasions, raids Madurai, and runs the medieval world's most ambitious price-control economy
bullets: Alauddin Khalji took the throne in 1296 by murdering his uncle Jalaluddin at a riverside meeting at Kara — one of the most efficiently executed coups in Sultanate history.
Between 1296 and 1306, Alauddin's reorganized army defeated six separate Mongol invasions from the Chagatai khanate, including a 1303 invasion that besieged Delhi itself but failed to take the city.

The Sultanate and Mamluk Egypt were the only two Islamic states to successfully resist Mongol absorption — the structural reason the Sultanate's Persianate Indo-Islamic culture was able to develop independently.

Malik Kafur, originally a Hindu eunuch slave from Gujarat, led three southern campaigns between 1308 and 1313 that raided as far south as Madurai (~1,400 miles from Delhi) and brought back the Koh-i-Noor diamond.

Alauddin set fixed government prices for all major commodities sold in the Delhi markets, enforced through a state apparatus of registered merchants, professional informants, and severe penalties for non-compliance.

The price-control system funded a massive standing army at low cost and is the most ambitious experiment in medieval state economic planning anywhere in the world — it collapsed within months of Alauddin's death in 1316.

The fall of the Sisodia fortress of Chittor in 1303 became the source of the legendary story of Padmini, the Rajput queen whose mass jauhar (collective suicide by fire) entered Hindu memory as the founding image of Rajput resistance.
The Khalji moment lasted thirty years and produced more sustained imperial achievement than the previous fifty years of the Mamluk dynasty had managed.

title: The Tughlaq Catastrophes
subtitle: Muhammad bin Tughlaq's brilliant disasters wreck the empire he inherited
bullets: Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq founded the dynasty in 1320 and built the massive new fortress city of Tughlaqabad — six and a half miles of cyclopean walls — which was abandoned within a few years of his death, possibly because it lacked a reliable water supply.

Muhammad bin Tughlaq was probably the most intelligent ruler the Sultanate ever produced — fluent in Persian and Arabic philosophy, mathematician, astronomer — but his reign was a sequence of catastrophes triggered by his own grand initiatives.

He ordered the entire population of Delhi to migrate 700 miles south to a new capital at Daulatabad in 1327 — the migration killed many along the route during a multi-year drought, and was reversed within a few years.

The 1330–1333 token currency experiment — brass and copper coins backed by the imperial treasury — was six centuries ahead of fiat money and collapsed when the empire could not prevent forgers from minting their own.

The 1329 Khurasan expedition raised an army of 370,000 troops, paid for them for a year, and then disbanded them without leaving Delhi when the political situation in Central Asia changed — draining the treasury for nothing.

The Bahmani Sultanate broke away from the Deccan in 1347, founded by the discontented Tughlaq officer Hasan Gangu as Alauddin Bahman Shah — and the Vijayanagara empire was founded the year before in 1336 by two re-converted brothers, Harihara and Bukka.

Firuz Shah Tughlaq's 37-year reign (1351–1388) stabilized what remained through cautious administration, public works including the West Yamuna Canal, and the founding of new cities at Firuzabad, Hisar, and Jaunpur.

Firuz Shah transported two Ashoka pillars to Delhi at enormous engineering effort — he could not read the Brahmi script (it would not be deciphered until James Prinsep in 1837) but he preserved the inscriptions for the modern world.

title: The Timurid Sack and the Sayyid Hangover
subtitle: Timur destroys Delhi in three days and the Sultanate becomes a city-state
bullets: Timur invaded India in late 1398 and entered Delhi unopposed on December 18 after defeating the Tughlaq sultan Mahmud Shah at the Battle of Loni.

Three days of looting and massacre killed perhaps 100,000 Delhi residents; Timur additionally ordered the mass execution of perhaps 100,000 Hindu prisoners his army had captured during the campaign south.
Timur left Delhi in late December 1398 with vast caravans of looted treasure and captured artisans — the Samarkand mosque at his capital was built largely by craftsmen taken from Delhi.
Khizr Khan, Timur's appointed governor of the Punjab, captured Delhi in 1414 and founded the Sayyid dynasty as nominal vassals of the Timurids — the dynasty ruled a city, not an empire.

While the Sayyid sultans held Delhi as a city-state, the rest of the subcontinent was ruled by the Bengal Sultanate, the Jaunpur Sultanate under the Sharqi dynasty, the Malwa Sultanate at Mandu, the Gujarat Sultanate, the Bahmani Sultanate in the Deccan, and Vijayanagara in the south.
Bengal under the Ilyas Shahi dynasty became one of the wealthiest Islamic states in the world, controlling the Indian Ocean trade through Chittagong and patronizing Bengali-language literature.

Vijayanagara at its peak under Krishnadevaraya controlled most of southern India, ruled a capital (modern Hampi) of 500,000 inhabitants, and fielded armies several times the size of the Lodi Sultanate that succeeded the Sayyids in 1451.

Babur, the Timurid prince who would conquer Delhi in 1526, was Timur's great-great-great-grandson and traced his Indian conquest as a kind of ancestral revisitation of his great-great-great-grandfather's expedition.

title: The Bhakti and Sufi Revolution
subtitle: Nizamuddin Auliya, Amir Khusrau, Kabir, Mirabai, Guru Nanak — the cultural synthesis that outlasted the dynasties
bullets: Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti settled at Ajmer in 1192 and founded the Indian Chishti Sufi order; his dargah at Ajmer remains the most visited Sufi shrine in the world, drawing roughly four million pilgrims a year of every faith.

Sheikh Nizamuddin Auliya, the most influential Chishti master of the Sultanate period, refused to attend the Sultanate court even when summoned — his Hanuz Dilli dur ast (Delhi is still far off) became permanent Hindi-Urdu proverbial wisdom.

Amir Khusrau served as court poet to seven successive sultans across fifty years, wrote half a million verses in Persian, pioneered Hindavi vernacular poetry, and is traditionally credited with founding the qawwali devotional song form.
Modern qawwali singers from Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan back through the Sabri Brothers perform in a tradition continuously practiced at the Nizamuddin Dargah for the past seven hundred years.

Kabir, the Banaras weaver-poet, wrote ferociously plain Hindavi verse mocking both Hindu Brahmin priestcraft and Muslim formalism, pointing past both to a single formless God whom he called Ram in a deliberately generic sense.

Mirabai, a Rajput princess of the Mewar royal family, wrote Krishna-devotional poetry framed through unmistakably female-voiced longing that became some of the most beloved Hindi devotional verse ever written.

Guru Nanak, born in 1469 in a small Punjabi village, founded the religion that became Sikhism on the principles of honest work, sharing through the langar communal kitchen, and meditative remembrance of the divine name.
The Hindavi spoken in the bazaars of Sultanate Delhi mixed Khariboli Hindi grammar with Persian-Arabic and Sanskrit vocabulary — the direct linguistic ancestor of both modern Hindi and modern Urdu.

title: The Lodi Afghans and the Provincial Sultanates
subtitle: the first non-Turkic dynasty, the breakaway sultanates, and Vijayanagara as Hindu counterweight
bullets: Bahlul Lodi, an Afghan chieftain from the Pashtun Lodi tribe and governor of Sirhind in the Punjab, took Delhi in 1451 from the last Sayyid sultan and founded the first non-Turkic dynasty of the Sultanate.

The Lodi political model was the Afghan tribal confederation rather than the Turkic mamluk system — Bahlul treated his Pashtun nobles as first-among-equals, sharing decision-making with a council of senior chiefs.
Sikandar Lodi (1489–1517), Bahlul's son, was the most capable Lodi sultan and founded the new city of Agra in 1504 — the future Mughal capital and site of the Taj Mahal.

By 1500, the political map was a patchwork of regional states — Bengal, Jaunpur, Malwa, Gujarat, the Bahmani Sultanate (about to fragment into five Deccan Sultanates), Vijayanagara — of which the Lodi Sultanate at Delhi was just one.

Vijayanagara at its peak under Krishnadevaraya (1509–1529) controlled most of southern India, ruled a population perhaps three or four times the size of the Lodi Sultanate, and was the unambiguously largest single state in the subcontinent.

The Lodi tombs in modern Delhi's Lodi Gardens — the octagonal tombs of Sikandar Lodi and Muhammad Shah Sayyid — are the immediate architectural ancestors of Humayun's Tomb and ultimately the Taj Mahal.

Ibrahim Lodi alienated his Pashtun nobility through harsh treatment, demanding the kind of formal subordination the older Turkic sultans had received, and triggering revolts including the consequential one by his uncle Daulat Khan Lodi in the Punjab.
Daulat Khan Lodi invited Babur from Kabul to invade Delhi in 1525, expecting Babur to defeat Ibrahim and then withdraw — Babur had no intention of doing so.

title: Panipat 1526 and the End
subtitle: Babur's matchlocks and field guns end the Sultanate and begin the Mughal Empire
bullets: On April 21, 1526, Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi's army of perhaps 100,000 with his own force of 12,000 to 25,000 at the First Battle of Panipat, 55 miles north of Delhi.

Babur's army deployed in the Ottoman wagon-fort tactic with chained baggage carts, matchlock musketeers firing through gaps in the cart line, and field cannons providing supporting fire — the first major use of coordinated gunpowder weapons on the Indian battlefield.
The Lodi heavy cavalry charge broke against Babur's defensive perimeter; Ibrahim Lodi died on the field along with perhaps 15,000 of his soldiers, and Babur entered Delhi unopposed three days later.

Babur's victory at Panipat is the same kind of event as Cortes's victory at Tenochtitlan five years earlier and Pizarro's victory at Cajamarca seven years later: a small, well-equipped, gunpowder-equipped invading army defeating a large pre-gunpowder defending army.

The Sultanate left behind the iqta-derived land-revenue system, the Persianate language of administration (Persian remained the official language of north Indian governance until 1837), the Indo-Islamic architectural tradition, and the framework of governing a Hindu majority through a Muslim military aristocracy.

The Hindavi-Rekhta language of the Sultanate's bazaars developed into modern Hindi and modern Urdu; the cuisines of north India and Pakistan including biryani, kebabs, naan, and samosas are substantially Sultanate-era developments.

Modern Delhi still contains every Sultanate dynasty's monuments — the Qutb Minar at Mehrauli, Tughlaqabad's six-mile walls, Firuzabad with its Ashoka pillar, the Lodi tombs in Lodi Gardens, the Nizamuddin Dargah with its continuous qawwali tradition.

The actual end of the Sultanate at Panipat was a moment of military-technological transition more than a moment of dynastic-cultural rupture — the Indo-Islamic-Persianate cultural-administrative-religious world the Sultanate had built continued largely uninterrupted under the Mughals.
